coredata.dc:title: High-efficient synthesis of bright yellow carbon quantum dots catalyzed by SnO2 NPs
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chang-ling
An, Bao-Li
Li, Jing
Wang, Xiao-Hong
Zhang, Ji-Ming
Xu, Jia-Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Green-yellow carbon quantum dots (CDs-2) were synthesized using 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid as carbon source with SnO2 nanoparticles (NPs) as a catalyst in one-step solvothermal method. The color of CDs-2 was tuned to be yellow (CDs-4) using m-phenylenediamine as both nitrogen source and a base. The pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine was greatly catalyzed by SnO2 NPs which were formed by hydrolysis of pentahydrate SnCl4 with n-butylamine or m-phenylenediamine as a base in the one-step solvothermal reaction. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields for CDs-2 and CDs-4 were reached 15.2% and 38.0% respectively at 180 °C for 2 h. The emission intensity of CDs-4 was not observed to decline over 35 days in ethanol dilute solutions. The catalysis of SnO2 NPs was further investigated and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Luminescence
Solvothermal synthesis
Catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the surface functional groups of CDs-4
The catalysis and the fluorescence color

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs-4.
(a) The TEM image of CDs-4, (b) the HRTEM image of CDs-4, (c) the FFT pattern of the HRTEM of CDs-4, (d) the size distribution of CDs-4.
Full-survey XPS spectrum of CDs-4 (a), high-resolution spectra for C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).
UV–vis spectra of the CDs in dilute ethanol solution.
Effect of material composition on fluorescence excitation spectra (left) and emission spectra (right).
Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.192 mg/ml CDs-4 with different excitation wavelengths.
(a) The excitation spectra of CDs-4 at different concentration, (b) the emission spectra of CDs-4 at different concentration of ethanol solutions.
The lifetime decay curve for CDs-4 at 568 nm.
The stability of the synthesized CDs-4 in dilute ethanol solution.
The fluorescence properties of the CDs.
The lifetime data for the synthesized CDs.

head.ce:title: High-efficient synthesis of bright yellow carbon quantum dots catalyzed by SnO2 NPs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green-yellow carbon quantum dots (CDs-2) were synthesized using 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid as carbon source with SnO2 nanoparticles (NPs) as a catalyst in one-step solvothermal method. The color of CDs-2 was tuned to be yellow (CDs-4) using m-phenylenediamine as both nitrogen source and a base. The pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine was greatly catalyzed by SnO2 NPs which were formed by hydrolysis of pentahydrate SnCl4 with n-butylamine or m-phenylenediamine as a base in the one-step solvothermal reaction. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields for CDs-2 and CDs-4 were reached 15.2% and 38.0% respectively at 180 °C for 2 h. The emission intensity of CDs-4 was not observed to decline over 35 days in ethanol dilute solutions. The catalysis of SnO2 NPs was further investigated and discussed.

Bright yellow carbon quantum dots have been synthesized by pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine in one–step solvothermal reaction at 180 °C for 2 h with SnO2 NPs as a catalyst.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm [1]. CDs have attracted comprehensive attention due to their distinctive properties in terms of good biocompatibility [2], good optical stability, low toxicity [3,4], and low cost [5–7]. They have been extensively used in luminescent molecular labelling, bioimaging, luminescent molecular sensors, etc [8–10]. Understanding the luminescence mechanism of CDs is important for regulation of their optical color. The luminescent mechanism for CDs is very complicated, and two kinds of luminescent mechanisms have been accepted by most of researchers [11]. The one mechanism is that the band gap states come from the conjugated π-domains of CDs, and the other is that the fluorescence emission comes from the surface defects of the CDs [12]. The CDs with blue, green and red fluorescent emission were obtained by controlling the graphitization degree of the carbon core and regulating the modification of functional groups on the surface [13]. Blue and green-yellow fluorescence could be tuned by controlling the degree of carbonization and the conjugate structure in the CDs [14]. Blue, green and yellow fluorescence could be obtained by incorporating N atoms in the graphene CDs and adjusting the conjugated structure of CDs in different solvents [15].

At present, the synthesis of efficient blue CDs has been already mature. However, the applications of blue CDs in biological fields are limited due to their weak tissue penetration and the interference of the spontaneous fluorescence from biological substrates [16]. Consequently, plenty of strategies have been used to promote the red shift of the emission wavelength of CDs. The polarity of solvent is an important factor. Polar solvent which could form hydrogen bonds with hydrophilic groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2) could regulate the emission wavelengths of CDs [17]. Simultaneously, acidic agents are often used to adjust the surface defects which determine the optical properties of CDs. Phosphoric acid was used to regulate the surface states of CDs, and yellow and red emissions were obtained [17]. Heteroatom doping is the most popular and effective way to promote the red shift of the emission wavelength of CDs. For example, N-doped CDs with red emission were synthesized by p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine and dopamine [18].

In addition, heteroatom doping can also improve the quantum yield of CDs. Blue color CDs with quantum yield of 83% were synthesized using resorcin and formamide as raw chemicals [19]. As the precursor, aromatic ammonia was used to prepare red color CDs with quantum yield of 84% [20].

There are various methods to prepare carbon quantum dots, and the reaction time has an crucial influence on the carbonization degree of CDs, which determines the luminescence properties and quantum yield of CDs [21]. The reaction time could be greatly shorten by microwave and microplasma synthesis method [22,23], while the CDs have low quantum yield together with wide range of size distribution and poor uniformity [24,25]. Therefore, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are still the most popular methods to synthesize CDs [26]. At present, the synthesis time is generally more than 8 h using hydrothermal and solvothermal methods to synthesize CDs [9,23]. CDs with narrow emission bands were synthesized using m-trihydroxybenzene as raw reagent, and the synthesis time was reduced much in 2–5 h by using concentrated sulfuric acid and concentrated hydrochloric acid as catalysts [27]. Regrettably, silica gel column chromatography was needed to separate the pure color components of the sample. Therefore, researchers are looking for easy and fast methods to synthesize CDs with high quantum yield.

Herein, bright yellow fluorescent CDs have been synthesized by one-step solvothermal method with 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine as raw chemicals. The synthesis time was reduced much by using SnO2 NPs as a catalyst, and the absolute quantum yield for CDs-4 reached 38% in 2 h at 180 °C. CDs-4 showed good stability in ethanol dilute solution, the emission intensity was not observed to decline over 35 days. Bright green-yellow fluorescent CDs (CDs-2) have been synthesized by using similar synthesis method as for CDs-4, and the absolute quantum yield for CDs-2 reached 15.2% in 2 h at 180 °C. The catalysis of SnO2 NPs for pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine was further investigated and discussed.

2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid (98%) was purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co. LTD, m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) (99.5%), SnCl4·5H2O (99%), ethyl alcohol (99.7%) and n-butylamine were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent co. LTD (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade and were used directly without further purification.

The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM-2010F). The test was carried out at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 with an Al anode as Kα source. The infrared spectra in the range of 4000-400 cm−1 were measured on a Thermo Nicolet AVATRA 370 infrared spectrophotometer by conventional KBr method. The ultraviolet–visible spectra were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Pulsing TU-1950) at room temperature. The excitation and emission spectra were measured by a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS 1000) with both the excitation slit and the emission slit of 0.8 nm. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs were recorded from Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with EPL-Visible 375 nm laser (10–12 s) as the excitation source light, and the lifetime data were obtained by auto calculation of the time decay curves by this instrument. The absolute quantum yields were also determined on Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with a Red PMT sphere, and all the quantum yield values were obtained by auto calculation by this instrument.

170.1 mg (1 mmol) 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and 350.1 mg (1 mmol) SnCl4·5H2O were dissolved in 20 ml ethyl alcohol, and the mixture solution was stirred at 60 °C for 1 h. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and it was kept at 180 °C for 2 h. The resulting solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. Some nano particles of tin oxide were formed by the hydrolysis of SnCl4·5H2O at 180 °C and the tin oxide particles could be easily removed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the solution was filtered under vacuum with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the larger nano carbon particles, and the soluble small molecules were removed by dialysis (molecular weight 3500 Da) in the dialysis bag. Violet fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs-1) were obtained.

Green-yellow color CDs were synthesized by the similar experiment as CDs-1. The pH value of the mixed solution of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid (170.1 mg, 1 mmol) and SnCl4·5H2O (350.1 mg, 1 mmol) was adjusted slowly by 400 μl n-butylamine (4 mmol) in ethanol solution. SnO2 NPs solution was formed by the completely hydrolysis of SnCl4·5H2O at 60 °C for 1 h with n-butylamine as a base. Then, the mixed solution was kept at 180 °C for 2 h. The Green-yellow color CDs solution was purified as the same method described for CDs-1. Bright green-yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs-2) were obtained. Compared with that for CDs-1, the pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid was efficiently catalyzed by the SnO2 NPs which could be easily removed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min.

Shallow yellow color CDs were synthesized by similar method as that for CDs-2 without SnCl4·5H2O. 1 mmol 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and 2 mmol m-PD were dissolved in 20 ml ethyl alcohol, and the solution was stirred at 60 °C for 1 h. The mixed solution was kept at 180 °C for 2 h. Then the shallow yellow color CDs solution was purified as the same method described for CDs-1. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min, there was only a little amount of precipitation. Shallow yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs-3) were obtained.

Yellow CDs were synthesized by similar method as that for CDs-2, only replacing n-butylamine by m-PD. Typical synthesis of yellow CDs is as follows. The pH value of the mixture solution of 1 mmol 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and 1 mmol SnCl4·5H2O was adjusted slowly by m-PD (108.1 mg, 2 mmol) ethanol solution. Hydrolysis of SnCl4·5H2O were completed slowly at 60 °C for 1 h by dropping m-PD solution as a base, and nano tin oxide particle solution was formed. Then, the mixed solution was kept at 180 °C for 2 h. Then, the yellow color CDs solution was purified as the same method described for CDs-1. Bright yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs-4) were obtained. The pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-PD was efficiently catalyzed by the nano tin oxide particles formed in the one-step reaction. The concentration of CDs-4 was determined to be about 23 mg/ml by evaporation of the solvent under vacuum. Bright yellow fluorescence was observed under radiation of a 365 nm lamp. Fig. 1 shows the reaction path for CDs-4 under the optimal synthesis condition.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the HRTEM of CDs-4, and the distribution of CDs-4 is uniform and nearly spherical at low resolution. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the HRTEM image is given in Fig. 2(c), the bright spots testify favourably the crystallinity of CDs-4. At higher resolution, the distinct lattice fringe of the nano carbon particles is 0.21 nm (Fig. 2(b)), which is associated with the (100) crystal surface of graphite [23,28]. Besides, Fig. 2(d) illustrates that the average diameter of CDs-4 is c. a. 4.56 nm. The HRTEM images for CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 are given in Fig. S1. Compared with those for CDs-4, the diameter for CDs-1 is relatively small and the crystallinity is very weak (Fig. S1(a)). The lattice fringe for CDs-1 is 0.33 nm, which is related to the (002) diffraction planes of graphite [29,30]. Compared with that for CDs-1, CDs-2 shows clear lattice fringe of 0.21 nm (Fig. S1(b)). The reason was attributed to that the pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid could be catalyzed by the nano SnO2 particles which were formed from the hydrolysis of SnCl4 with n-butylamine as a base. Compared with that for CDs-4, the particles size for CDs-3 is also small (Fig. S1(c)), and its lattice fringe is 0.33 nm. For CDs-3, the pyrolysis of the reactants was not complete without the catalysis of nano SnO2 particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the surface functional groups of CDs-4
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR of CDs-4 is given in Fig. 3 . The wide peak from 2500 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 was ascribed to the characteristic stretching vibration absorption of –COOH [31], and the strong peak at 1627 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration absorption of CO further confirmed the –COOH group. The wide and strong peak around at 3360 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, and the medium peak at 1312 cm−1 is ascribed to the in-plane bending vibration of O–H. Peaks at 1143 cm−1 and 1013 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration absorption of C–O group [32] further confirmed the –OH group. As a result of the absorption overlapping for –NH2 and –OH, two weak peaks at 3363 cm−1 and 3348 cm−1 were found to be over the wide and strong peak at around 3360, they were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the –NH2 respectively. The peak at 821 cm−1 due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of N–H further confirmed the existence of –NH2. Several weak peaks from 3059 to 3211 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption of C–H on aromatic rings. The peak at 1481 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the CC [33,34]. The FTIR spectrum indicates that abundant –COOH and –OH groups with some –NH2 groups are on the surfaces of CDs-4. The IR spectrum for CDs-2 was similar with that for CDs-4, just the characteristic absorption peaks for –NH2 group was not found (Fig. S2). The IR spectrum for CDs-3 is similar with that for CDs-4, just the absorption intensities for the peaks in region of 2500–3600 cm−1 are weaker than those for CDs-4 (Fig. S2). The IR spectrum for CDs-1 is obviously different with that for CDs-2, indicating the difference of their structure and surface groups.

The XPS spectrum indicates the presence of elements of C1s (286 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (532 eV) on the surfaces of CDs-4 (Fig. 4 (a)). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be divided into three peaks, and they are attributed to -C-C/CC (284.5 eV), -C-N/C–O (285.2 eV) and –CO (288.9 eV) respectively (Fig. 4(b)). The high-resolution XPS peak for N1s can be divided into three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.4 eV, and they are attributed to –CN, amino N and C–N–C (Fig. 4(c)). The XPS peak for O1s can be divided into two peaks at 530.6 eV and 532.2 eV, and they are ascribed to –CO and -C-OH/C–O–C respectively (Fig. 4(d)). The intensity for O1s is about ten times of that for N1s, it indicates that there are abundant –COOH and –OH groups, and some –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs-4 [35]. The XPS results are consistent with that from the IR spectrum. However, no characteristic peak for element of Sn was found. The XPS spectra for CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 are given in Fig. S3. The XPS spectrum for CDs-1 composed of two peaks of C1s and O1s (Fig. S3 (a1)). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be divided into two peaks, and they are attributed to -C-C/CC (283.9 eV), -C-O (285.2 eV) (Fig. S3 (a2)). The high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be divided into two peaks, and they are attributed to –CO (530.02 eV), -C-O/-C-O-C (531.7 eV) (Fig. S3 (a3)). The XPS full-survey spectrum for CDs-2 is given in Fig. S3 (b1). The C1s can be divided into two peaks of –CC (284.0eV) and -C-N/-C-O (285.1eV) (Fig. S3 (b2)). The O1s for CDs-2 (Fig. S3 (b3)) is similar with that for CDs-1. The high-resolution spectrum for N1s composes of one peak, which is attributed to -C-N (Fig. S3 (b4)). The full-survey XPS spectrum for CDs-3 is given in Fig S3 (c1), and its high-resolution spectra for C1s, O1s and N1s are similar with those for CDs-4 (Fig. S3 (c2), (c3) and (c4)).

Fig. 5 gives the UV–vis spectra of the synthesized CDs. Strong absorption centered at 273 nm and weak absorption in region of 400–500 nm was observed for CDs-1, and the peak at 273 nm was ascribed to the absorption of π-π* electron transition [36,37]. As for CDs-2, the absorption of the π-π* transition shifted to 287 nm, and the absorbance increased over 1 times compared with those for CDs-1. The reason was attributed to that larger conjugated structure for CDs-2 was produced in the pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid, which was efficiently catalyzed by the SnO2 NPs formed in the one-step reaction with n-butylamine as a base. Moreover, the absorption around at 380 nm for CDs-2 was increased c. a. 3 times compared with that for CDs-1. The reason was ascribed to that more interface defect states for CDs-2 were formed compared with that for CDs-1. The absorption spectrum for CDs-3 was similar with that for CDs-1, just the absorption of the π-π* transition shifted to 293 nm. The reason was attributed to that larger conjugated structure for CDs-3 was formed in the pyrolysis of both 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine. The absorbance around at 294 nm for CDs-4 was increased c. a. 2 times compared with that for CDs-3, and the absorbance around at 427 nm for CDs-4 was increased c. a. 8 times compared with that for CDs-3. The reason was ascribed to that the pyrolysis of both 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine, which was efficiently catalyzed by the SnO2 NPs formed in the one-step reaction with m-phenylenediamine as both a base and N source. In addition, more interface defect states for CDs-4 were formed compared with those for CDs-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalysis and the fluorescence color
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the quantum yield for CDs-4 was greatly affected by the raw material molar ratio of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid versus m-phenylenediamine (Fig. S4 and Table S1). The optimum molar ratio of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoi acid versus m-PD was 1:2, and the quantum yield for CDs-4 reached 38%. The optimum reaction temperature was 180 °C as the reaction time was set for 2 h together with the optimum ratio of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoi acid versus m-PD (Fig. S5, Table S2). The optimum reaction time was 2 h as the reaction temperature was set at 180 °C together with the molar ratio of 1:2 for 2,4,6-trihydroxybenzoic acid versus m-PD (Fig. S6, Table S3).

The emission spectra for CDs-4 were recorded under excitation wavelengths from 360 nm to 560 nm (Fig. 7 ). The maximum emission wavelength centered at 568 nm, and the emission spectra were independent with the excitation wavelengths. The phenomenon of emission independent with excitation was attributed to the homogeneous surface structure with good crystallinity of CDs-4 [38], and similar phenomenon has been found for a green color carbon quantum dots [39]. Generally, the maximum emission wavelengths for CDs red shifted to longer wavelengths with increased excitation wavelengths [40]. Thus, the emission behavior for CDs-4 is distinctive among the emission performance for common CDs. The composition and the interface defects are needed to be investigated continuously to give rational understanding for the emission mechanism for CDs-4.

The excitation and emission intensities increased as the synthesized CDs-4 (23 mg/ml) were diluted 20 (1.15 mg/ml), 40 (0.575 mg/ml), 60 (0.383 mg/ml), 80 (0.288 mg/ml), 100 (0.230 mg/ml) times with ethanol, and the intensities started to decline as the synthesized CDs-4 was diluted 120 times (0.192 mg/ml) (Fig. 8 ). The maximum emission wavelength red shifted gradually from 568 nm to 576 nm as the concentration of CDs-4 increased from 0.230 mg/ml to 1.15 mg/ml (Fig. 8(b)). Interestingly, the excitation spectra for CDs-4 are composed of 3 peaks at around 338 nm, 380 nm and 520 nm, and the three peaks were named as Peak D1, D2 and D3 (Fig. 8(a)). As the concentration of CDs-4 increased from 0.230 mg/ml to 1.15 mg/ml, Peak D1 red shifted from 336 nm to 345 nm and Peak D3 red shifted from 525 nm to 548 nm. As maximum excitation wavelengths for excimers’ absorption have been observed to shift to longer wavelengths with the increased concentrations of the samples [41,42], thus, the Peak D1 and Peak D3 were ascribed to the excimer absorption of CDs-4. The Peak D2 blue shifted from 380 nm to 365 nm as the concentration increased from 0.230 mg/ml to 1.15 mg/ml. The blue shift of the maximum excitation wavelength for peak D2 might related to the structure change of the surface groups with increased concentration of CDs-4. However, it needs to be investigated further to find definite evidence.

The lifetimes were calculated from the emission decay curves for CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4, they were detected at 405 nm, 545 nm, 568 nm and 568 nm respectively with an EPL-UV 375 nm laser (10−12 s) as excitation source. All the decay curves are fitted perfectly in second exponential decay equation by the auto calculation of FLS 1000, and the results are given in Table 2 . The average lifetime for CDs-4 was c. a. 7 times of that for CDs-3. Generally, the non-radiative transition probability is smaller, the quantum yield is higher together with longer lifetime. Thus, the lifetime data for CDs-3 and CDs-4 were consistent with their fluorescence quantum yield data showed in Table 1. The average lifetime for CDs-2 was c. a. 1.56 times of that for CDs-1, and the result was consistent with their quantum yield data. The lifetime data further confirmed that SnO2 NPs could efficiently catalyze the pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine. The decay curve for CDs-4 is given in Fig. 9 , and other decay curves for CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 are given in the supporting information of Fig. S7.

Stability is necessary for practical application of carbon quantum dots [43]. The intensities at 568 nm for CDs-4 were recorded under excitation light of 525 nm using same instrument at same determine condition over 35 days, and the fluorescence stability curve was given in Fig. 10 . The fluorescence intensity was not observed decline over 35 days.

Bright yellow luminescent carbon quantum dots have been synthesized with 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid used as carbon source and m-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source. Compared to that of the literatures, the synthesis time was reduced much by SnO2 NPs as a catalyst, and 23 mg/ml CDs-4 could be easily synthesized in one-step reaction. The quantum yield of the synthesized yellow carbon quantum dots reached 38% in 2 h. SnO2 NPs were confirmed to have great catalysis for pyrolysis of 2, 4, 6-trihydroxybenzoic acid and m-phenylenediamine. The emission intensity for CDs-4 in ethanol dilute solution was not observed to decline over 35 days.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, the Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (19ZR1418900), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0102900), Chinese Academy of Sciences (KLIFMD201704), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61671284; U1704255), and the Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Peak Discipline Construction program), the Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government. The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis
J. Mater. Chem. A.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Luminescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emission nitrogen, boron, sulfur co-doped carbon dots for “on-off-on” fluorescent mode detection of Ag+ ions and L-cysteine in complex biological fluids and living cells
Anal. Chim. Acta
